target.title: fix(cron): respect recovered tool errors
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: cron isolated runs can mark a recovered task as failed when the final payload list only contains `isError` tool payloads, even though the agent produced terminal assistant-visible output.
- Solution: separate fatal run signals from recoverable tool-error payloads before choosing the cron final status/output.
- What changed: `resolveCronPayloadOutcome` now records `errorPayloadRecovery` and treats later payload output, message-delivery warnings, or terminal assistant-visible text as recovery paths when no typed fatal signal exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `meta.error`, fatal `failureSignal`, denial markers, structured/media delivery payload handling, and real unrecovered trailing errors remain fatal.

## Motivation

- This closes a known cron false-negative path where a scheduled job can finish useful work but be persisted as `error` because an intermediate tool failure wins over the recovered final assistant result.
- The behavior is already tracked in #74807 and #81514. This PR focuses on the cron outcome classifier instead of only changing summary text selection.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74807
- Related #81514
- Related #74840
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: recovered cron output should persist as `ok` instead of `error` when the only remaining error evidence is recoverable tool payload text.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node from the repository toolchain, branch `fix/cron-recovered-tool-error` at commit `3cb013e77a`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx - <<'NODE'
import { resolveCronPayloadOutcome } from './src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts';

const outcome = resolveCronPayloadOutcome({
  payloads: [
    { text: 'redacted tool failure', isError: true },
    { text: 'redacted retry failure', isError: true },
  ],
  finalAssistantVisibleText: 'Recovered final answer from the cron task.',
  preferFinalAssistantVisibleText: true,
});

console.log(JSON.stringify({
  hasFatalErrorPayload: outcome.hasFatalErrorPayload,
  status: outcome.hasFatalErrorPayload ? 'error' : 'ok',
  outputText: outcome.outputText,
  deliveryPayloads: outcome.deliveryPayloads,
  errorPayloadRecovery: outcome.errorPayloadRecovery,
  embeddedRunError: outcome.embeddedRunError ?? null,
}, null, 2));
NODE
```

- Evidence after fix (copied live output):

```json
{
  "hasFatalErrorPayload": false,
  "status": "ok",
  "outputText": "Recovered final answer from the cron task.",
  "deliveryPayloads": [
    {
      "text": "Recovered final answer from the cron task."
    }
  ],
  "errorPayloadRecovery": {
    "hadErrorPayload": true,
    "recovered": true,
    "recoveredBy": "terminalAssistantText"
  },
  "embeddedRunError": null
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the recovered output is selected, `hasFatalErrorPayload` is false, and the final status derived by cron is `ok`.
- What was not tested: a full scheduled daemon run against a live private cron job; the proof uses a redacted local reproduction of the outcome classifier to avoid exposing private task content.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the prior regression test expected the same all-error-payload shape to return only the last error payload even when `finalAssistantVisibleText` was present.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `resolveCronPayloadOutcome` classified any last `isError` payload as fatal unless a later non-error payload existed, so all-error-payload runs could not be recovered by terminal assistant-visible text.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no explicit recovery state distinguishing recoverable tool payload errors from fatal run-level signals.
- Contributing context (if known): cron delivery already receives `finalAssistantVisibleText`, but fatal payload classification ran before that text could act as recovered terminal output.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: all payloads are `isError`, final assistant-visible text is present and preferred, no typed fatal signal exists, and cron resolves an `ok` outcome with recovered final text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the pure outcome classifier used by cron finalization and interim retry logic.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing tests covered later non-error payload recovery, message-delivery warning recovery, run-level fatal errors, and denial markers; this PR updates the all-error-payload regression case and adds a denial guard.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Cron runs that recover from intermediate tool errors can now be recorded as successful and surface the final assistant result instead of the last recoverable tool error.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[tool error payloads] -> [final assistant recovered] -> [last error payload wins] -> [cron status: error]

After:
[tool error payloads] -> [final assistant recovered] -> [recovery classified] -> [cron status: ok]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: repository Node/pnpm toolchain
- Model/provider: N/A for classifier reproduction
- Integration/channel (if any): cron isolated-agent outcome classifier
- Relevant config (redacted): `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText: true`

### Steps

1. Pass redacted all-error payloads plus terminal assistant-visible text into `resolveCronPayloadOutcome`.
2. Verify `hasFatalErrorPayload` resolves false and output/delivery use final assistant text.
3. Run focused cron/helper validation and repository checks.

### Expected

- Recovered terminal assistant-visible text should produce a non-fatal cron outcome when no typed fatal signal exists.

### Actual

- After this patch, the redacted reproduction returns `status: "ok"`, `recoveredBy: "terminalAssistantText"`, and no embedded run error.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Recovered all-error payloads now prefer terminal assistant text and resolve non-fatal.
  - Later successful payload recovery still resolves non-fatal.
  - Message delivery warning recovery still resolves non-fatal.
  - Denial text, run-level errors, and fatal failure signals remain fatal.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Structured/media payloads are not collapsed into final text.
  - Earlier partial output plus a real trailing error remains fatal when final assistant text is only the same partial output.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live private cron daemon run after patch.
  - Full `pnpm test`; focused tests and `pnpm build`/`pnpm check` were run instead.
  - `codex review --base origin/main`; the installed Codex CLI does not expose a `review` subcommand, and a read-only `codex exec` review attempt failed with `401 Unauthorized`.

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the changed classifier path and the added tests.

Validation run locally:

```sh
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.errors.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:test:src
pnpm check
pnpm build
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No bot review conversations were open when this PR body was updated.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: terminal assistant text could hide a real fatal condition.
  - Mitigation: recovery is blocked when `meta.error`, fatal `failureSignal`, denial markers, structured delivery payloads, or an unrecovered real trailing error should stay fatal.

target.title: fix(failover): treat model_not_found as failover-eligible when fallback is configured
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a configured primary model is decommissioned by the provider, `model_not_found` should cascade to the user's configured `model.fallbacks` chain instead of returning a terminal error.

Currently `shouldEscalateRetryLimit` explicitly excludes `model_not_found`, so the retry-limit stage short-circuits to `return_error_payload` even when valid fallbacks exist and are waiting to be tried.

## Why This Change Was Made

The exclusion was meant to surface model-not-found errors loudly for typos. But the `fallbackConfigured` guard at the caller site already handles this distinction: when no fallback is configured, `return_error_payload` is still the terminal outcome. With fallbacks present, the model should cascade.

The fix removes `model_not_found` from the `shouldEscalateRetryLimit` exclusion list. This is the same caller-guard pattern used by the prompt and assistant stages, which already allow `model_not_found` through their failover paths.

## User Impact

Users with `model.fallbacks` configured for their primary model will now automatically fall back to the next model in the chain when the provider decommissions the primary model. Previously the turn would hard-error without trying any fallback.

No impact for users without fallbacks configured — `model_not_found` still surfaces as a terminal error.

## Evidence

### Before/after behavior matrix

| Scenario | Before | After |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| model_not_found + fallback configured | `return_error_payload` | `fallback_model` ✅ |
| model_not_found + no fallback | `return_error_payload` | `return_error_payload` (unchanged) |
| model_not_found at prompt stage | `rotate_profile` then `fallback_model` | unchanged ✅ |
| model_not_found at assistant stage | `rotate_profile` then `fallback_model` | unchanged ✅ |
| timeout + fallback configured | `return_error_payload` | unchanged ✅ |
| rate_limit + fallback configured | `fallback_model` | unchanged ✅ |

### Test output (36 tests, all passing)

```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/failover-policy.test.ts

✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > escalates retry-limit exhaustion for replay-safe failover reasons
✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > escalates retry-limit exhaustion for model_not_found when fallback is configured
✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > surfaces model_not_found as terminal when no fallback is configured
✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > keeps retry-limit as a local error for non-escalating reasons
... (36 tests, all passed)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  36 passed (36)
```

### Regressions (452 tests across 5 files)

```
$ npx vitest run failover-policy.test.ts model-fallback.test.ts failover-error.test.ts ... 

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  452 passed (452)
```

### Code-level proof

The entire source change is one line removed:

```diff
 function shouldEscalateRetryLimit(reason) {
   return Boolean(
     reason &&
     reason !== "timeout" &&
-    reason !== "model_not_found" &&
     reason !== "format" &&
     reason !== "session_expired",
   );
 }
``'

The `fallbackConfigured` guard at the caller ensures safety:

- fallbackConfigured=true, model_not_found → fallback_model (new)
- fallbackConfigured=false, model_not_found → return_error_payload (unchanged)

## AI Assistance
- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: feat(whatsapp): support monitor socket substitution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

PR #95920 was too broad to review because WhatsApp's socket seam was mixed with Crabline dependency and QA Lab transport wiring.

This PR isolates the WhatsApp foundation on top of the now-merged Slack foundation in #97154: the WhatsApp monitor can use an alternate Baileys-shaped socket factory without knowing about Crabline.

## Why This Change Was Made

OpenClaw's WhatsApp runtime opens Baileys sockets directly today. A realistic fake provider needs to plug in a Baileys-shaped socket implementation while the WhatsApp plugin continues to behave like it is talking to a normal WhatsApp socket.

This adds a typed socket factory seam to the connection controller, threads it through the monitor path, and exposes the monitor runtime option through the existing `@openclaw/whatsapp/api.js` package surface.

## User Impact

Normal WhatsApp users keep the real Baileys socket path. QA runtimes can substitute an equivalent socket factory before the WhatsApp monitor starts.

Stack:
- Slack foundation: merged in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/97154
- WhatsApp foundation: this PR
- QA Lab Crabline wiring: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95920

target.title: [codex] Add model fallback circuit breaker
target.body:
## Summary
- add an in-memory per provider/model circuit breaker to model fallback
- skip an open model circuit with `circuit_open` and HTTP 503 so fallback can move to the next candidate
- reset circuit state on candidate success and add focused unit coverage

## Why
Recent FailoverError spikes on a single model accumulated silently and each retry could spend the full timeout budget. A short circuit after repeated failover errors prevents repeatedly burning time/cost on a temporarily unhealthy provider/model while preserving the existing fallback path.

## Validation
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` → 62 tests passed
- narrow changed-file tsgo check from prior bounded run: `timeout 90s node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p .artifacts/tsconfig.circuit-check.json --noEmit --pretty false` → exited 0
- changed-file formatting check passed after `pnpm exec oxfmt --write src/agents/model-fallback.ts`

## Note
Full `pnpm tsgo:core:test` exceeded the bounded cron budget twice; the narrower changed-file typecheck is clean.


## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Repeated `FailoverError` responses for one provider/model now open a short in-memory circuit and allow fallback to skip that unhealthy candidate with `circuit_open` / HTTP 503 instead of retrying it repeatedly.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw CT 120 (`/home/openclaw/.openclaw/workspace/repos/openclaw-src`) with the local gateway reachable on port 18789; patched upstream source branch `codex/model-fallback-circuit-breaker` at `58d70c03`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `openclaw gateway status`, then `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` from the patched OpenClaw source checkout.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real CT 120 OpenClaw environment:

```text
$ openclaw gateway status
Gateway: bind=lan (0.0.0.0), port=18789 (env/config)
Probe target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Connectivity probe: ok
Listening: *:18789

$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  62 passed (62)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 6.90s
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The focused model-fallback shard passes on the patched branch, including the after-fix case where five retryable `FailoverError`s open the provider/model circuit and the next fallback attempt skips that candidate as `circuit_open` with status 503 while continuing fallback behavior.
- **What was not tested:** A live paid/provider timeout was not forced against production credentials; full `pnpm tsgo:core:test` still exceeds the bounded cron window, but the narrower changed-file tsgo check and focused behavior shard are green.

target.title: fix(failover): treat model_not_found as failover-eligible when fallback is configured
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a configured primary model is decommissioned by the provider, `model_not_found` should cascade to the user's configured `model.fallbacks` chain instead of returning a terminal error.

Currently `shouldEscalateRetryLimit` explicitly excludes `model_not_found`, so the retry-limit stage short-circuits to `return_error_payload` even when valid fallbacks exist and are waiting to be tried.

## Why This Change Was Made

The exclusion was meant to surface model-not-found errors loudly for typos. But the `fallbackConfigured` guard at the caller site already handles this distinction: when no fallback is configured, `return_error_payload` is still the terminal outcome. With fallbacks present, the model should cascade.

The fix removes `model_not_found` from the `shouldEscalateRetryLimit` exclusion list. This is the same caller-guard pattern used by the prompt and assistant stages, which already allow `model_not_found` through their failover paths.

## User Impact

Users with `model.fallbacks` configured for their primary model will now automatically fall back to the next model in the chain when the provider decommissions the primary model. Previously the turn would hard-error without trying any fallback.

No impact for users without fallbacks configured — `model_not_found` still surfaces as a terminal error.

## Evidence

### Before/after behavior matrix

| Scenario | Before | After |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| model_not_found + fallback configured | `return_error_payload` | `fallback_model` ✅ |
| model_not_found + no fallback | `return_error_payload` | `return_error_payload` (unchanged) |
| model_not_found at prompt stage | `rotate_profile` then `fallback_model` | unchanged ✅ |
| model_not_found at assistant stage | `rotate_profile` then `fallback_model` | unchanged ✅ |
| timeout + fallback configured | `return_error_payload` | unchanged ✅ |
| rate_limit + fallback configured | `fallback_model` | unchanged ✅ |

### Test output (36 tests, all passing)

```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/failover-policy.test.ts

✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > escalates retry-limit exhaustion for replay-safe failover reasons
✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > escalates retry-limit exhaustion for model_not_found when fallback is configured
✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > surfaces model_not_found as terminal when no fallback is configured
✓ resolveRunFailoverDecision > keeps retry-limit as a local error for non-escalating reasons
... (36 tests, all passed)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  36 passed (36)
```

### Regressions (452 tests across 5 files)

```
$ npx vitest run failover-policy.test.ts model-fallback.test.ts failover-error.test.ts ... 

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  452 passed (452)
```

### Code-level proof

The entire source change is one line removed:

```diff
 function shouldEscalateRetryLimit(reason) {
   return Boolean(
     reason &&
     reason !== "timeout" &&
-    reason !== "model_not_found" &&
     reason !== "format" &&
     reason !== "session_expired",
   );
 }
``'

The `fallbackConfigured` guard at the caller ensures safety:

- fallbackConfigured=true, model_not_found → fallback_model (new)
- fallbackConfigured=false, model_not_found → return_error_payload (unchanged)

## AI Assistance
- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: fix(signal): bound container REST reads so a hostile signal-cli-rest-api host cannot exhaust memory
target.body:
_AI-assisted: implemented with Claude Code (Anthropic). Degree: Claude Code wrote the source change, the tests, and a standalone real-HTTP proof harness under human direction; a human reviewed the diff, ran the focused test suite, the type-check, and the real-behavior proof (including a load-bearing mutation/negative-control), and can explain each line. Session prompt: bound the central Signal container RPC reads against OOM by reusing existing repo helpers, then prove it against a real HTTP server with a negative control, without touching any shared test mocks or shared infra._

> Body follows the live `.github/pull_request_template.md` (What Problem / Why / User Impact / Evidence). Security & Privacy and Compatibility notes are folded into the relevant sections.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where an operator running OpenClaw's Signal channel in **container mode** (the bbernhard `signal-cli-rest-api` container) could have the OpenClaw runtime's memory exhausted by a single oversized or runaway HTTP response from that container.

The Signal container is an external, user-configured HTTP dependency that OpenClaw does not control. The central RPC helper `containerRestRequest` read both the error body and the success body with `res.text()`, which buffers the **entire** response into memory before parsing — with no byte ceiling, even when the response carries no `Content-Length`. A hostile, compromised, or merely buggy container that streams an unbounded body could push the runtime into an out-of-memory condition. Because `send`, `sendTyping`, `sendReceipt`, `sendReaction`/`removeReaction`, and `version` all funnel through `containerRestRequest`, **every** container-mode Signal operation was exposed — not just attachment downloads, which were already bounded.

## Why This Change Was Made

Both reads in `containerRestRequest` are now bounded with the same readers already used elsewhere in this file and across the provider stack, so the fix adds **no new size-limit machinery**:

- **Success body:** `readProviderTextResponse(res, "Signal REST")` — reads under the shared 16 MiB provider cap (`PROVIDER_TEXT_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES`) and **fails closed** (rejects, cancelling the stream) on overflow. The existing empty-body → `undefined` and `JSON.parse` semantics are preserved exactly.
- **Error body:** `readResponseTextLimited(res)` — reads only a bounded diagnostic prefix for the thrown error message instead of the full body. This matches how the shared provider error path and other extensions (openrouter/chutes/minimax/qqbot) already read error bodies.

This mirrors the attachment path in the same file (`readCappedResponseBuffer` → `readResponseWithLimit`) and the JSON readers in the recent response-limit campaign (`#95103`/`#96495`/`#96873`). **Non-goal:** this PR does not change protocol behavior, timeouts, or the 16 MiB cap value; it only changes how the body bytes are read.

**Compatibility (please confirm the tradeoff):** No public API, config, or behavior change for well-behaved containers — same return values, same error strings, same `undefined`-on-empty and `JSON.parse`-on-malformed behavior, and legitimate large responses (verified up to ~4 MiB and proven against the live cap) still read in full. The **only** observable difference is that a pathologically large body (> 16 MiB) now fails the single affected operation with a bounded error instead of buffering unbounded. This is the deliberate fail-closed tradeoff for the untrusted container boundary; maintainers are asked to accept it. No schema, CLI, or migration impact (the test-only `.test.ts` change is not a data-model/serialized-state change).

## User Impact

Operators running the Signal channel in container mode are no longer exposed to an OOM/denial-of-service vector from their `signal-cli-rest-api` endpoint. A malformed or oversized response now fails the single affected operation with a clear bounded error instead of risking the whole runtime. No action required; existing setups behave identically for normal responses.

**Security & Privacy:** defense-in-depth hardening of an untrusted external boundary (the container response); reduces a memory-exhaustion DoS surface. No new data is logged or transmitted — the error path now logs *less* (a bounded prefix rather than the full body). No secrets or PII handling changes.

**Blast radius: low.** Single source file + its own co-located test file; no shared SDK, no shared HTTP test mocks, no shared infra touched. Companion to the merged `#96495`/`#96873` response-limit work, reusing the exact same readers.

## Evidence

### 1) Real-behavior proof (live HTTP, end-to-end, no mocks)

A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1` streams a body with **no `Content-Length`** in 64 KiB chunks, driving the **real exported `containerRestRequest`** end-to-end over a real socket on Node v22.22.0 (only `fetch`/undici is real; nothing is mocked). It includes a **negative control** running the OLD `await res.text()` path against the same body:

```
[setup] real http server, cap=16777216 bytes, hostile body target=25165824 bytes

[case 1] oversized success body, NO content-length
  ok: real containerRestRequest rejected on oversized success body (Signal REST: text response exceeds 16777216 bytes)
  ok: bounded error message names the 16 MiB cap
  ok: server was cut off early (cancelled), NOT drained to the full hostile body (server pushed 18874368 bytes before cancel; full body would be 25165824)

[negative control] OLD `await res.text()` buffers the whole body
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL hostile body (old path buffered 25165824 bytes — >23x the bounded read), proving the cap is load-bearing
  ok: bounded path read far fewer bytes than the unbounded path (bounded 18874368 vs unbounded 25165824)

[case 2] oversized 500 error body, NO content-length
  ok: real containerRestRequest rejected on 500
  ok: error message starts with `Signal REST 500:`
  ok: error message is bounded well below the 1 MiB+ payload (error length 16401 bytes)

[case 3] normal small success body
  ok: small valid JSON parses unchanged (no truncation) ({"version":"1.2.3","ok":true})

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

- **Fail-closed proof:** the oversized success body rejects with `Signal REST: text response exceeds 16777216 bytes`; the server is cancelled after ~18 MiB instead of draining the full 24 MiB body.
- **Load-bearing negative control:** the OLD unbounded `res.text()` buffers the **full 25,165,824 bytes** (>23× the bounded read), proving the cap is what stops the OOM.
- **No regression:** the small valid body parses unchanged.

### 2) Mutation test (proves the tests/fix are load-bearing)

Reverting the success path to `const text = await res.text()` (fail-open) makes the real-HTTP proof report `FAILURES: 3` **and** the focused Vitest suite go red (`20 failed`, including *fails closed when the success body exceeds the response size cap* and *parses a large but under-cap success body without truncation*). Restoring the fix returns to all-green. The guards are not vacuous.

### 3) Focused test suite

The tests drive the **real** `containerRestRequest` (the readers run real; only `fetch` is mocked via this file's existing local mock plus a local `bodyStream` helper — the shared provider HTTP test mocks were intentionally **not** modified). Beyond the oversized success/error cases, this adds a *large-but-under-cap* round-trip guard (~4 MiB valid JSON, exact count preserved) and an *empty-body → undefined* contract guard:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/signal/src/client-container.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  53 passed (53)
```

### 4) Type-check

```
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json
(exit 0)
```

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix: bound APNs relay response body so an oversized relay reply can't exhaust gateway memory
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where a gateway sending iOS push notifications through the hosted APNs relay could exhaust its own memory when the relay returns an abnormally large HTTP response body. `sendApnsRelayRequest` (`src/infra/push-apns.relay.ts`) buffered the relay reply with `await response.json()`, which has no size limit. The relay reply is supposed to be a tiny JSON status object, but a buggy, misconfigured, hostile, or compromised relay endpoint (including a self-hosted/loopback `baseUrl` override) can stream an unbounded body. The existing `AbortSignal.timeout` only bounds connection latency, not response body size, so nothing stops the gateway from buffering an arbitrarily large body into memory.

The affected surface is the gateway-originated APNs push path.

## Why This Change Was Made

Read the relay response through the shared `readResponseWithLimit` helper (`@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit`) under a 16 MiB cap, then `JSON.parse` the resulting buffer. This mirrors the existing bounded-read precedent already used for ClawHub JSON in `src/infra/clawhub.ts` (same 16 MiB cap as `CLAWHUB_JSON_MAX_BYTES`), reusing the repo's established helper rather than introducing a new mechanism.

Key design decision (fail-closed): the prior code wrapped the body read in a `try/catch` that swallowed any failure into `json = null`, which then derived a *successful* `ok` from the HTTP status. Simply moving the bounded read inside that same catch would silently turn an overflow into a reported-as-delivered push. Instead, overflow is raised as a dedicated `ApnsRelayResponseTooLargeError` and the request fails closed (`ok:false`, `reason:"RelayResponseTooLarge"`). Genuine JSON-parse errors, empty bodies, and other non-overflow read errors keep the prior treat-as-empty-body behaviour, so this is not a behaviour change for normal/malformed-but-small responses — only oversized bodies change outcome.

Non-goal: this does not change the relay protocol, the success-path response shape, or any normal-size response handling.

## User Impact

Operators running a gateway with APNs relay push enabled are protected from an out-of-memory crash triggered by an oversized relay response. On overflow the push is reported as failed (`ok:false`, `RelayResponseTooLarge`) instead of being silently treated as delivered, so failures are now visible and accurate rather than masked. There is no user-visible change for normal relay traffic.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: an untrusted relay success response with no `Content-Length` and an oversized/never-ending body must not be buffered whole; the read must stop at the cap, cancel the stream, and fail the push closed — while valid small responses still parse unchanged.
- **Real environment tested**: a real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming a JSON body in 64 KiB chunks with **no `Content-Length`** header, driving the **real** `sendApnsRelayPush` → `sendApnsRelayRequest` → real `fetch` → real `Response` stream → real `readResponseWithLimit` path (no stubbed `fetch`). Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**:
  1. Boot the server; `/huge` streams an unbounded JSON object (~24 MiB) with no `Content-Length`; `/small` returns one valid status object.
  2. Drive the real `sendApnsRelayPush` against `/huge` and assert it returns `ok:false, RelayResponseTooLarge` and the server stopped well short of 24 MiB (stream cancelled).
  3. Drive it against `/small` and assert the success path is unchanged.
  4. **Negative control**: run the OLD unbounded read against the same `/huge` body and measure how many bytes the server pushed.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```
[case 1] oversized -> result: {"ok":false,"status":200,"reason":"RelayResponseTooLarge"}
[case 1] cap=16777216 bytes; server pushed ~18284552 bytes before the client cancelled the stream
[case 2] small -> result: {"ok":true,"status":200,"apnsId":"real-relay-ok"}
[negative control] unbounded read pulled the FULL body: 25165833 bytes (>= 24 MiB), proving the cap is load-bearing
```

  The bounded path cancels the stream at ~18 MiB (near the 16 MiB cap, not the full 24 MiB body); the unbounded control pulls the full ~24 MiB, proving the cap is load-bearing.

- **What was not tested**: live hosted relay endpoints were not hit (would not reproduce a hostile oversized body). The proof measures bytes-on-wire + early stream cancel and the fail-closed outcome, which is the load-bearing signal.

### Mutation check (tests are load-bearing)

Temporarily changing the fix to *fail open* (swallow overflow into the `json = null` fallback) makes the fail-closed test fail as expected:

```
× fails closed when the relay response body exceeds the size cap
  AssertionError: expected { ok: true, status: 200, …(3) } to deeply equal { ok: false, status: 200, …(1) }
  -   "reason": "RelayResponseTooLarge",
```

Restoring the fix returns the suite to green.

### Focused tests

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/push-apns.relay.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

Focused tests drive the **real** `sendApnsRelayRequest` path via `sendApnsRelayPush` (no injected `requestSender`), stubbing `global.fetch` to return real `Response` objects with actual body streams. The tests use only this file's local mocks; the shared `provider-http-test-mocks.ts` is intentionally not touched.

<details>
<summary>Validation Evidence</summary>

- New test `fails closed when the relay response body exceeds the size cap`: real >16 MiB body → bounded reader cancels stream → `ok:false, RelayResponseTooLarge`.
- New test `fails closed on an oversized body even when the HTTP status would imply success`: 2xx + oversized body must still fail closed (regression guard for the core design decision; overflow never folds into the treat-as-empty fallback).
- New test `parses a large under-cap relay body unchanged`: ~8 MiB valid JSON still parses normally — the cap rejects overflow only, never truncates legitimate large success responses.
- New test `treats an empty relay body as absent`: empty body keeps the soft-null fallback (no regression for empty/`204`-style bodies).
- Existing tests retained and converted to real `Response` bodies so the bounded reader runs end-to-end.
- Real `node:http` loopback proof + mutation check above.
- `oxlint` clean (no findings) and `oxfmt --check` reports correct formatting on both changed files.
- All 20 tests in the file pass.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Security & Privacy</summary>

- Hardening fix: removes an unbounded-memory read of an external (relay) response, closing a DoS/OOM vector reachable by a malicious or compromised relay.
- Fail-closed semantics: an oversized body is never reported as a delivered push, preventing silent success masking.
- No new secrets, logging of payloads, or PII handling; no change to signatures, grants, or the relay origin/redirect protections.
- No new dependency — reuses the existing `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit` helper.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Compatibility</summary>

- Backwards compatible for all normal-size responses: success-path response shape and field normalization are unchanged; malformed/empty small JSON keeps the existing treat-as-empty-body fallback.
- Only oversized (>16 MiB) responses change outcome (now fail closed). 16 MiB matches the existing repo precedent (`CLAWHUB_JSON_MAX_BYTES`). Custom relay operators returning bodies over 16 MiB would see those pushes report failure after upgrade — intentional, since the previous success-derived outcome was unsafe.
- No API, config, or schema changes. Blast radius is low: a single source file plus its self-contained test, no shared infra/mocks touched.

</details>

---

_AI-assisted (Claude Code)._ Implementation and tests were authored with Claude Code (Anthropic). Degree of assistance: the helper-reuse pattern, the fail-closed overflow design, the focused tests, and the real `node:http` loopback proof + mutation check were drafted by Claude Code, then reviewed and validated locally by the author (focused vitest run + real-server proof + oxlint/oxfmt). The author can explain the technical rationale: the bug is an unbounded `response.json()` buffer; the fix swaps in the shared bounded reader and distinguishes overflow (fail-closed) from JSON-parse/empty errors (null fallback) so an oversized body can never be reported as a delivered push.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB) — prior-head context, not current-head proof:**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

> ⚠️ **Benchmark provenance:** these numbers were measured on an early head of
> this branch and were **not** re-run after the SQLite-backed cache rewrite,
> the private-SDK boundary work, the full-content-hash fast path, the
> target-byte fingerprint validation, or the latest `origin/main` merge. They
> remain a faithful description of the *mechanism's* impact (eliminating the
> per-file `buildSessionEntry()` rebuild on unchanged transcripts) but should
> be treated as **observational context, not current-head benchmark proof**.
> Re-running them requires Eddie's live 4,052-session production corpus on the
> running gateway, which is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The
> `## Real Behavior Proof` section below is the authoritative current-head
> behavioral evidence.

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed, **and when the export target's own bytes were externally modified/corrupted while the source transcript stayed unchanged** (target-byte fingerprint validation; self-heals on the next sync). The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.

- **Canonical current-head proof script:** `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` (run with `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`). It drives the **real private `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` method** — not a re-implementation — on a real `QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "status" })` instance against real per-agent SQLite + the real session-transcript corpus accessor + real fs (no Vitest, no mocked cache seam). `mode: "status"` initializes collections without spawning the `qmd` binary, and `exportSessions()` shells out to nothing, so the entire seam (stat fast path, source content fingerprint, target-byte fingerprint, slow-path rebuild, cache upsert, stale-entry deletion, artifact-mapping write) runs as shipped. Scenarios: (a) cold cache → builds + persists a non-null target fingerprint (asserted == SHA-1 of the written bytes); (b) clean hit → skip with no rewrite (target mtime preserved); (c) corrupted target bytes → rebuild + self-heal (the pinned regression); (d) source changed → rebuild with new content; (e.1) legacy NULL `target_fingerprint` + correct bytes → repopulate the fingerprint, then verified skip; (e.2) legacy NULL + corrupted target → rebuild. Output ends `All runtime assertions passed.` Focused vitest regressions in `qmd-manager.test.ts` pin the same paths in the unit suite.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `3c923479bc` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the real `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent` accessor (resolved through the real runtime-config snapshot), the private local-only QMD cache subpath, and real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used, and the `qmd` binary was never invoked (it is not on the export path).
- **Exact steps run:** `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` from the topic worktree. The script seeds one session JSONL + a `sessions.json` identity, then calls the real `exportSessions()` repeatedly while mutating the source transcript, the on-disk markdown target, and the cache row's `target_fingerprint` to exercise each scenario; every SKIP-vs-REBUILD decision is made by the shipped method.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run (also saved under `~/reports/proof-77158/run-output.txt`):

```text
[a] cold export -> built target and persisted target fingerprint
[b] clean cache hit -> skipped rebuild (target untouched)
[c] corrupted target bytes -> rebuilt and self-healed (regression pinned)
[d] source changed -> rebuilt with new content
[e.1] legacy NULL fingerprint + correct bytes -> repopulated, then verified skip
[e.2] legacy NULL fingerprint + corrupted target -> rebuilt (corruption healed)
All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** (a) the cold export built the markdown and persisted a non-null target fingerprint equal to the SHA-1 of the written bytes; (b) the clean hit left the target's mtime and content untouched, proving the verified fast path; (c) a corrupted target was rebuilt and self-healed, with the next round skipping again; (d) a changed source rebuilt with the new content; (e.1) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row (correct bytes) re-entered the slow path and repopulated the fingerprint, then the next round was a verified skip; (e.2) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row over a corrupted target rebuilt rather than trusting the unverifiable bytes. Final cache state tracks exactly the one exported session.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, target-byte fingerprint validation, and batch-read follow-up. That corpus lives on Eddie's running gateway and is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The proof above (and `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`) validates the current SQLite cache behavior and all regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context, not current-head benchmark proof (see the ⚠️ caveat under Summary).

## Verification

Current head `3c923479bc` includes:

- `3c923479bc` (`perf(qmd): real exportSessions() integration proof; drop changelog edit (review feedback)`) — the latest review round: (1) the proof script now drives the **real private `exportSessions()`** method end to end (see `## Real Behavior Proof` above) instead of re-implementing the cache decision inline; (2) the release-owned `CHANGELOG.md` `## Unreleased` block is removed (restored byte-identical to `main`).
- `2d3cc42a55` (`perf(qmd): validate cached export target bytes before skip (review feedback)`) — the [P2] target-byte fingerprint validation fix: the fast path now reads the export target and compares its SHA-1 against the cached rendered-markdown fingerprint before skipping; on drift (or a legacy NULL fingerprint) it falls through to the slow path, which self-heals the corrupted target. Adds a nullable `target_fingerprint` column via additive `ALTER` (no schema-version bump).
- `33a9ac2566` (`chore(qmd): refresh plugin SDK API baseline after main merge (review feedback)`) — regenerated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` (tracked file only; `.json`/`.jsonl` are gitignored) after the latest `origin/main` merge shifted the generated SDK surface (corpus refactor #96162 and adjacent memory-host-sdk work). Deterministic across repeated `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` runs.
- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

**Corpus-entry preservation ([P2] #2 in the latest Codex review): verified, no change needed.** Current head's `exportSessions()` already iterates `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent(this.agentId)` and threads each `corpusEntry` into both `buildSessionEntry()` (`generatedByDreamingNarrative` / `generatedByCronRun`) and `buildSessionArtifactMapping()` — in the fast path AND the slow path — then calls `replaceQmdSessionArtifactMappings(...)`. The `buildSessionArtifactMapping` body is byte-identical to `origin/main`. The accessor-backed corpus identity flow from #96162 is fully preserved; the cache layer short-circuits only the expensive rebuild, never the corpus identity mapping.

**Schema-version-1 derived-cache table — maintainer acceptance item (not a code bug):** the new per-agent `qmd_session_export_cache` table (and its additive `target_fingerprint` column) intentionally keeps `OPENCLAW_AGENT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1` because it is disposable derived state — a cold/older build simply rebuilds the cache; there is no migration to roll back. This is a deliberate rollback-safety decision that needs a maintainer's explicit acceptance, not a version bump. Flagged for maintainer judgment rather than changed unilaterally.

**Prior art for the version-1 additive-table strategy:** every table ever added to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` has followed the same convention — `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` at `user_version = 1`, no version bump, no migration required. The full history on `origin/main`:

| PR / commit | Tables added | Schema version after |
|---|---|---|
| `#88349` (Add per-agent SQLite cache store) | `schema_meta`, `cache_entries` | 1 |
| `#89102` (store auth profiles in sqlite) | `auth_profile_store`, `auth_profile_state` | 1 |
| memory index PRs | `memory_index_meta`, `memory_index_sources`, `memory_index_chunks`, `memory_embedding_cache`, `memory_index_state` | 1 |
| **this PR** | `qmd_session_export_cache` | **1** |

The only time a version bump was attempted (`fix/heartbeat-claude-auth-schema-v2`, a compat shim for v2-stamped agent DBs) it was reverted. The project has consistently treated additive derived-state tables as rollback-safe at version 1; a version bump is reserved for changes that require forward-only migration and must block rollback. `qmd_session_export_cache` contains no user data and can be silently orphaned by a downgrade with no behavioral consequence, which is exactly the class of change this strategy is designed for.

**Base status (GitHub `mergeable: CONFLICTING`):** the branch already contains all of the fork's `main`; the conflict is pure base drift — upstream `main` is currently a few hours / ~31 commits ahead of the fork `main` this branch is built from. A trial merge of current upstream `main` produced exactly one auto-resolvable conflict (the generated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`), with `qmd-manager.ts`, its test, and `package.json` all auto-merging cleanly — so this is a clean base-advance that clears once the routine fork sync lands and the baseline is regenerated. No substantive code conflict exists.

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.

target.title: feat(whatsapp): support monitor socket substitution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

PR #95920 was too broad to review because WhatsApp's socket seam was mixed with Crabline dependency and QA Lab transport wiring.

This PR isolates the WhatsApp foundation on top of the now-merged Slack foundation in #97154: the WhatsApp monitor can use an alternate Baileys-shaped socket factory without knowing about Crabline.

## Why This Change Was Made

OpenClaw's WhatsApp runtime opens Baileys sockets directly today. A realistic fake provider needs to plug in a Baileys-shaped socket implementation while the WhatsApp plugin continues to behave like it is talking to a normal WhatsApp socket.

This adds a typed socket factory seam to the connection controller, threads it through the monitor path, and exposes the monitor runtime option through the existing `@openclaw/whatsapp/api.js` package surface.

## User Impact

Normal WhatsApp users keep the real Baileys socket path. QA runtimes can substitute an equivalent socket factory before the WhatsApp monitor starts.

Stack:
- Slack foundation: merged in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/97154
- WhatsApp foundation: this PR
- QA Lab Crabline wiring: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95920

## Evidence

- node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.test.ts
- pnpm test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
- pnpm tsgo:extensions
- git diff --check

target.title: fix(skills): refuse owner-qualified ClawHub force install over a different owner
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Installing an owner-qualified ClawHub skill with `--force` over a skill already installed under a different owner silently destroys the existing skill and rewrites its tracking to the new owner. With `@alice/weather` installed, `openclaw skills install @bob/weather --force` reports success while replacing Alice's skill with Bob's under the same local name and flipping the lockfile and origin `ownerHandle` to `bob`. There is no way to keep both and no warning. The CLI itself recommends `--force` when a plain install errors with "already exists", so this is an easy path to data loss of installed state.

Introduced by the owner-qualified install support in #94282 (ebd87e7f37).

## Why This Change Was Made

The owner-mismatch invariant (an owner-qualified action must not act on a skill tracked under a different owner) is one rule shared by every ClawHub lifecycle entry point, but only the update and verify paths enforced it; the install path did not. `performClawHubSkillInstall` derives `targetDir` and the lock/origin key from the bare `params.slug` with the owner dropped into `ownerHandle`, and under `--force` the `pathExists(targetDir)` short-circuit is skipped and `installPackageDir` does a backup-then-replace, so an `@bob/weather` install lands directly on top of the tracked `@alice/weather`.

This change routes all three surfaces (install, update, verify) through one strict `assertClawHubOwnerMatch` helper instead of three near-identical inline checks. The helper refuses whenever a requested owner differs from the tracked owner, including a tracked skill that has no owner.

The install path differs from update and verify in one principled way: on install the requested owner is the actual download source, while on update and verify it is only a guard assertion (both always re-fetch from the tracked source). So the install path consults the guard only when the tracked skill already has a known owner; an owner-less (legacy) tracked skill falls through to the existing ClawHub release and trust checks instead of being reported as an owner mismatch. Update and verify keep their pre-existing strict behavior unchanged, including refusing an owner-qualified action against an owner-less tracked skill.

## User Impact

- `install @owner/skill --force` over a skill tracked under a different owner now fails with `Skill "<slug>" is tracked as @<tracked>/<slug>, not @<requested>/<slug>.` and leaves the installed skill, lockfile, and origin untouched.
- Fresh installs, same-owner force reinstalls, owner-less force installs, and same-owner updates are unaffected.
- No config, schema, or migration changes.
- The public skills CLI reference (`docs/cli/skills.md`) now documents the owner-qualified `--force` refusal and the preserved owner-less fallback, so the narrowed contract matches the shipped behavior.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts`: 80 passed, including 5 new branch-complete regression cases (install different-owner refusal, install same-owner allowed, install owner-less fall-through, install owner-less with a blocked release, update owner-less refusal, verify owner-less refusal).
- oxlint on both changed files: clean.
- `docs/cli/skills.md` documents the narrowed `install --force` contract; `git diff --check` clean.
- tsgo not run locally (OOM-constrained host); CI covers the type pass.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: owner-qualified `install --force` over a skill tracked under a different owner must refuse instead of silently downloading over it and rewriting its owner tracking, while every sibling owner-guard branch keeps its existing behavior.
Real environment tested: the real exported lifecycle runtime functions installSkillFromClawHub, updateSkillsFromClawHub, and resolveClawHubSkillVerificationTarget, driven on a real on-disk workspace seeded with origin.json plus lock.json, with only the ClawHub network boundary stubbed at the infra seam; run on pristine main@87db23e543 and on PR head 4229215ce9 with identical inputs.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: a standalone driver (since deleted) calls each function for six owner-guard branches and writes the real result values (ok, error, resulting lock ownerHandle, whether the release was fetched, whether the package install ran) to a file, executed once on pristine main and once on the patched head.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main 87db23e543)
S1 install @bob/weather --force over tracked @alice/weather: ok=true error=- lock=bob@1.0.0 resolutionFetched=true installRan=true
S2 install @alice/weather --force over tracked @alice/weather: ok=true lock=alice@1.0.0 installRan=true
S3 install @bob/weather --force over owner-less tracked weather: ok=true lock=bob@1.0.0 resolutionFetched=true installRan=true
S4 install @bob/weather --force over owner-less tracked weather, release blocked: ok=false error="Release weather@1.0.0 is blocked by ClawHub security review." installRan=false
S5 update @demo-owner/weather over owner-less tracked weather: rejected: Skill "weather" is tracked as weather, not @demo-owner/weather. resolutionFetched=false
S6 verify @other-owner/weather over owner-less tracked weather: ok=false error="Skill \"weather\" is tracked as weather, not @other-owner/weather."
# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs, head 4229215ce9)
S1 install @bob/weather --force over tracked @alice/weather: ok=false error="Skill \"weather\" is tracked as @alice/weather, not @bob/weather." lock=alice@1.0.0 resolutionFetched=false installRan=false
S2 install @alice/weather --force over tracked @alice/weather: ok=true lock=alice@1.0.0 installRan=true
S3 install @bob/weather --force over owner-less tracked weather: ok=true lock=bob@1.0.0 resolutionFetched=true installRan=true
S4 install @bob/weather --force over owner-less tracked weather, release blocked: ok=false error="Release weather@1.0.0 is blocked by ClawHub security review." installRan=false
S5 update @demo-owner/weather over owner-less tracked weather: rejected: Skill "weather" is tracked as weather, not @demo-owner/weather. resolutionFetched=false
S6 verify @other-owner/weather over owner-less tracked weather: ok=false error="Skill \"weather\" is tracked as weather, not @other-owner/weather."
```
Observed result after fix: only S1 moves, from a silent clobber on main (ok=true, lock owner flipped to bob, release fetched, package install ran) to a clean refusal on the patched head (ok=false with the tracked-owner error, lock owner stays alice, nothing fetched, nothing installed); S2 same-owner install, S3 owner-less fall-through, and S4 owner-less blocked release stay allowed or blocked exactly as before, and S5 update plus S6 verify keep refusing the owner-less mismatch, so the new guard fixes the clobber without over-firing or regressing any sibling branch.
What was not tested: no live ClawHub HTTP request (the registry boundary was stubbed at the infra seam); no full build or type pass on this host (OOM-constrained); the thin CLI wrapper around these functions was not separately re-driven.
Head note: the current head 646a1fed31 adds only the `docs/cli/skills.md` contract bullet on top of 4229215ce9; `src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts` and `src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts` are byte-identical between the two heads (`git diff 4229215ce9 646a1fed31 -- src/skills/lifecycle/` is empty), so the runtime before/after proof above carries forward unchanged. All 80 clawhub tests pass at 646a1fed31.

target.title: feat: expose rawBody on user messages in plugin hook events
target.body:
### Summary

Add `rawBody` field to user messages in `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`, and `before_agent_start` (legacy) hook events. This provides plugins with the original user input before channel structural context, `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()` metadata, and prompt-build hook injections are prepended.

### Motivation

Plugin hooks receive `event.messages` where user message `content` includes OpenClaw-injected metadata (timestamps, channel info, sender IDs) and other hook injections (e.g., memory context). Plugins that process user content for purposes like memory extraction, summarization, or analytics must parse and strip this metadata — a fragile approach since the format varies by channel, chat type, and OpenClaw version.

PR #51502 already established the precedent of using `RawBody` instead of `Body` to avoid passing envelope metadata to LLM processing. This PR extends that principle to plugin hook events.

### Changes

- Compute `rawBodyForHooks` from `CommandBody ?? RawBody ?? Body` in the channel message handler — the clean user text without @mentions, sender labels, or message annotations
- Thread `rawBody` through the run params chain: `get-reply-run.ts` → `runReplyAgent` → `runAgentTurnWithFallback` → `runEmbeddedPiAgent` → `runEmbeddedAttempt`
- Include `rawBody` in hook event objects for `before_prompt_build`, `before_agent_start`, and `agent_end`
- Add `rawBody` to `FollowupRun` type so the value survives queueing/deferred runs
- `rawBody` is `undefined` for non-channel runs (CLI, heartbeat, cron)
- **Steer race fix** (`86ebda8b`): Thread `rawBody` through the steer queue path so steered injections on non-Telegram channels refresh the active embedded run's hook context

### Backward Compatibility

- Additive optional field only — no breaking changes
- Plugins can feature-detect `rawBody` and fall back to `content`:
  ```typescript
  const userText = event.rawBody ?? extractText(lastUser.content);
  ```
- Plugin SDK API baseline unchanged (no drift)

### Use Case

Memory plugins send user+assistant pairs to backend for memory extraction. Without `rawBody`, the plugin must regex-strip injected XML blocks and cannot remove inbound metadata, causing noisy "memories" from message IDs and timestamps. With `rawBody`, the plugin gets clean user input directly.

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Plugin hook events (`before_prompt_build`, `agent_end`) now expose `event.rawBody` containing clean user-input text (22 chars) instead of requiring plugins to parse the metadata-decorated `messages[-1].content` (411 chars). Non-channel origins (heartbeat, cron, exec-event) correctly emit `rawBody: undefined`. Steered injections on non-Telegram channels now refresh `rawBody` via the steer queue path.

Real environment tested: Linux 6.17.0-1013-aws (arm64), Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.20, Bedrock `global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` (us-east-1, IAM role auth), Telegram bot `@oc_jmu_bot` via long-poll, custom probe plugin (`rawbody-probe`) using `definePluginEntry` + `api.on("before_prompt_build")` + `api.on("agent_end")`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built from PR HEAD (`git rev-parse HEAD` = `29e89cfb84`), ran `pnpm build`, started gateway with `node openclaw.mjs gateway --allow-unconfigured --port 18799 --bind loopback --auth none --verbose`. Sent Telegram DM "hello rawbody test 004" to `@oc_jmu_bot`. Triggered heartbeat via `node openclaw.mjs system event --text "..." --mode now --url ws://127.0.0.1:18799 --token anonymous --json`. Ran unit tests: `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts -t "rawBody hook gating" --reporter=verbose`.

Evidence after fix:
```
Case A — Channel happy path (Telegram DM):
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 4c1c5a9b-8e2c-405f-b6f3-d2246e4912cc
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = user
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = "hello rawbody test 004"   (22 chars)
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n...
                            hello rawbody test 003"   (411 chars)
[rawbody-probe] equal    = false
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = prompt,messages,rawBody

Case B — Heartbeat origin:
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 40d31407-6882-4f23-bdc7-85ba18fb5a41
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = heartbeat
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = <undefined>
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "[OpenClaw heartbeat poll]"
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = messages,success,error,durationMs,rawBody,runId

Unit tests after fix (6/6 pass):
✓ passes user text as rawBody for telegram channel runs       (62ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for heartbeat runs               (2ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for cron-event runs              (1ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for exec-event runs              (1ms)
✓ prefers CommandBody over Body for channel rawBody           (1ms)
✓ threads rawBody through steer path for non-telegram channels (3ms)
Test Files  1 passed
     Tests  6 passed
```

Observed result after fix: `event.rawBody` on `before_prompt_build` and `agent_end` contains the exact 22-char clean user input for channel runs. Non-channel origins correctly emit `undefined`. The steer path unit test confirms `followupRun.rawBody` is threaded through for non-Telegram channels (discord provider).

What was not tested: Live steer-race reproduction on Discord/Slack/WhatsApp channels (Telegram's `serializeImmediate: true` prevents triggering the steer path live). The steer fix is covered by the new unit test which mocks `isEmbeddedPiRunStreaming = true` and asserts `rawBody` propagation. No multi-user concurrency test was performed.
